hints: A separation process that exploits differences in boiling points
How crude oil is separated into petrol, diesel, and kerosene at a refinery
The mixture is heated; the more volatile component vaporises first and is condensed separately
Fractional distillation uses a column to separate components with close boiling points
Also used to produce spirits — the basis of every whisky and vodka production process
A substance that speeds up a chemical reaction without being consumed
Lowers the activation energy of a reaction
Your car's catalytic converter uses platinum and palladium to clean exhaust gases
Enzymes are biological versions — highly specific and incredibly efficient
Heterogeneous catalysts are in a different phase from the reactants; homogeneous are in the same phase
A fluid's resistance to flow
Honey and molasses are highly viscous; water and ethanol are not
Decreases with temperature for most liquids — cold honey pours slower than warm honey
Measured in pascal-seconds (Pa·s) or centipoise (cP)
Critical in designing pipelines, pumps, and mixing equipment in chemical plants
A device that transfers heat between two fluid streams without mixing them
Found in power plants, refineries, HVAC systems, and food processing
The driving force is temperature difference — heat flows from hot to cold
Common designs: shell-and-tube, plate-and-frame, and double-pipe
The log mean temperature difference (LMTD) is the key design parameter
The study of the rates at which chemical reactions proceed
Tells engineers how big a reactor needs to be for a given production rate
Rate depends on concentration, temperature, pressure, and catalyst presence
The Arrhenius equation relates rate constant to temperature
Reaction order describes how rate depends on reactant concentration: r = k[A]^n
A mass transfer process where a component from a gas is taken up by a liquid
Used in gas purification — removing CO₂ or H₂S from natural gas or flue gas
Occurs in a packed or tray column where gas and liquid contact each other
The driving force is concentration difference between phases
Distinct from adsorption — absorption is bulk uptake into a liquid; adsorption is surface-based
The science governing energy transformations in chemical systems
Determines whether a reaction is feasible and how much heat it releases or absorbs
Enthalpy (H), entropy (S), and Gibbs free energy (G) are its core quantities
A negative ΔG means a reaction is spontaneous under given conditions
The first and second laws underpin all process design — you can't violate them
An accounting of all material entering and leaving a system
Based on conservation of mass — what goes in must come out or accumulate
The first step in designing any chemical process
For a steady-state system: mass in = mass out (no accumulation)
Extended to include reactions: mass in + mass generated = mass out + mass consumed
The vessel in which a chemical reaction takes place
Design must account for kinetics, heat transfer, mixing, and safety
Three ideal models: batch, CSTR (continuous stirred tank), and PFR (plug flow)
A CSTR is well-mixed — concentration is uniform throughout
A PFR has no axial mixing — composition changes continuously along the length
The state where forward and reverse reaction rates are equal
The reaction hasn't stopped — both directions proceed at the same rate
Described by an equilibrium constant K — large K means products dominate
Le Chatelier's principle: disturb the equilibrium and it shifts to counteract the disturbance
Temperature, pressure, and concentration changes all shift equilibrium position
The removal of a solvent (usually water) from a solution by vaporisation
Used to concentrate solutions in food, pharmaceutical, and chemical industries
Driven by supplying heat — the solvent boils off, leaving behind concentrated product
Multiple-effect evaporation reuses steam vapour from one stage to heat the next
Distinct from drying — evaporation deals with liquid solutions, not solid-wet material
The reduction in pressure as a fluid flows through a pipe, valve, or packed bed
Caused by friction between the fluid and the pipe wall, and turbulence
Must be accounted for when sizing pumps and compressors
Increases with flow velocity, pipe length, and pipe roughness
Calculated using the Darcy-Weisbach equation: ΔP = f(L/D)(ρv²/2)
A separation and purification technique where a solute forms solid crystals from solution
Used to produce salt, sugar, pharmaceuticals, and fertilisers
Triggered by cooling the solution, evaporating solvent, or adding an anti-solvent
Crystal size and purity depend on nucleation and growth rates
Recrystallisation — dissolving and re-crystallising — is the classic lab purification method
A large molecule made of repeating smaller units (monomers) bonded together
Plastics, rubber, fibres, and many biological molecules are all this
Polyethylene, PVC, nylon, and polystyrene are common engineering examples
Properties depend on chain length, branching, and cross-linking
Two main synthesis routes: addition polymerisation and condensation polymerisation
A scale measuring the acidity or alkalinity of a solution
Ranges from 0 (most acidic) to 14 (most alkaline), with 7 being neutral
Each unit represents a tenfold change in hydrogen ion concentration
Critical in fermentation, water treatment, and pharmaceutical manufacturing
Defined as −log₁₀[H⁺], where [H⁺] is the molar concentration of hydrogen ions
A separation process using the preferential solubility of a compound in one solvent over another
Shaking an aqueous mixture with an organic solvent in a separating funnel is the lab version
Industrial scale uses mixer-settlers or extraction columns
The distribution coefficient (K_d) describes how the solute partitions between phases
Used to recover metals from ores, purify pharmaceuticals, and process nuclear fuel
Separation of solids from liquids or gases by passing them through a porous medium
The medium retains particles above a certain size while the fluid passes through
Industrial examples: rotary drum filters, filter presses, and membrane systems
Driving force can be pressure difference, gravity, or centrifugal force
Distinct from sieving — filtration can remove particles far smaller than visible pores
A rapid exothermic reaction between a fuel and oxygen
The energy source for most power generation, heating, and transportation
Complete combustion of hydrocarbons produces CO₂ and H₂O
Incomplete combustion produces CO, soot, and unburned hydrocarbons
Stoichiometric combustion uses exactly the right amount of air — excess air improves completeness
A process that occurs without heat exchange with the surroundings
The system's temperature changes come only from internal energy changes
Rapid compression of gas is approximately adiabatic — diesel engine ignition exploits this
The adiabatic flame temperature is the maximum temperature a fuel can reach on combustion
From Greek: 'not passable' — heat cannot pass through the system boundary
A reactor type where solid particles are suspended by an upward-flowing fluid
The solid behaves like a liquid — it can flow and mix vigorously
Excellent heat and mass transfer due to intimate gas-solid contact
Used in coal combustion, fluid catalytic cracking (FCC), and pharmaceutical coating
Named for the fluid-like behaviour of the solid particle bed when gas velocity exceeds minimum fluidisation velocity
The gradual destruction of a material through chemical or electrochemical reaction with its environment
Steel rusting is the most familiar example — iron oxidises in the presence of water and oxygen
Costs the global economy an estimated 3–4% of GDP per year
Controlled by coatings, cathodic protection, material selection, or inhibitors
Galvanic corrosion occurs when two dissimilar metals are in electrical contact in an electrolyte
A pollution control device that removes contaminants from industrial exhaust gases
Gas is passed through or over a liquid that absorbs the pollutant
Used to remove SO₂ from power plant flue gas (flue gas desulfurisation)
Also used to remove acid gases, dust, and odorous compounds
The wet scrubber is the most common type — the polluted gas contacts water or a reagent solution
A measure of how much of the desired product is formed relative to by-products
High selectivity means less waste and simpler downstream separation
Distinct from conversion — conversion measures how much reactant is consumed
Often a trade-off with conversion: conditions that maximise one may reduce the other
Defined as moles of desired product formed / moles of reactant consumed by all reactions
An accounting of all energy entering and leaving a system
The thermal equivalent of a mass balance — based on conservation of energy
Used to size heat exchangers, determine utility requirements, and assess process efficiency
Includes sensible heat, latent heat, and heat of reaction
For steady state: energy in + energy generated = energy out + energy accumulated (= 0)
The removal of moisture from a solid or semi-solid material
One of the most energy-intensive unit operations in chemical processing
Involves both heat transfer (to evaporate moisture) and mass transfer (moisture leaving the solid)
Types include spray dryers, drum dryers, fluidised bed dryers, and freeze dryers
Drying rate has two phases: constant rate (surface drying) and falling rate (internal diffusion controls)
The process of bringing different materials into intimate contact to achieve homogeneity
Critical for reactions, heat transfer, and suspension of solids
Impeller type (axial vs radial flow) and speed determine mixing intensity
Scale-up from lab to plant is notoriously difficult — power per unit volume changes
Poor mixing leads to hot spots, incomplete reactions, and inconsistent product quality
A device that automatically releases pressure from a vessel when it exceeds a set limit
The last line of defence against catastrophic overpressure failure
Mandatory on pressurised vessels, boilers, and process equipment
Opens at the set pressure and reseats when pressure drops below it
Sizing follows ASME or API standards; the consequences of undersizing are severe
The quantitative relationship between reactants and products in a chemical reaction
Based on the law of conservation of mass and balanced chemical equations
The limiting reagent is the reactant that runs out first and limits the yield
Theoretical yield is the maximum product possible based on limiting reagent
Essential for calculating feed rates, reactor sizing, and raw material costs
A separation process using a semipermeable barrier to selectively pass certain species
Reverse osmosis uses it to desalinate seawater — applying pressure to force water through
Driving forces can be pressure, concentration gradient, or electrical potential
Types include microfiltration, ultrafiltration, nanofiltration, and reverse osmosis
No phase change required — typically more energy-efficient than distillation for some separations
A vertical vessel filled with structured or random packing material to improve gas-liquid contact
Used in distillation, absorption, and stripping operations
Packing (Raschig rings, Pall rings, structured metal sheets) provides large surface area
Lower pressure drop than tray columns — preferred for vacuum distillation
Performance described by HETP (Height Equivalent to a Theoretical Plate)
